result:
The morning light came through the window in that particular way that makes everything look like a memory, even as it’s happening. I was sitting at the kitchen table, the one with the scratched surface from years of plates and elbows and homework assignments, and I was thinking about the last time I saw my father. Not the last time in the hospital, with the machines and the quiet beeps, but the last time he was really himself, standing in this same kitchen, making coffee with too much sugar, humming a song he could never remember the name of.

He had a habit of tapping his fingers on the counter, a little rhythm that seemed to come from nowhere. I used to find it irritating, that constant tapping, like a leaky faucet of sound. Now I’d give anything to hear it again, just once, to sit in that annoyance and let it wash over me, knowing he was there. Grief is strange that way. It’s not the big moments that undo you; it’s the tiny, insignificant details that you never thought to catalogue. The way he folded his newspaper. The specific brand of mints he always carried. The sound of his footsteps on the stairs, a slight drag on the left foot from an old injury he never talked about.

I remember one afternoon when I was maybe twelve, he took me to a hardware store for no reason other than he needed a new screwdriver. To him, a hardware store was a kind of museum, each aisle a gallery of potential and repair. He’d walk slowly, reading the backs of packages, explaining the difference between a Phillips head and a flathead, the magic of a properly sized washer. I was bored, of course, desperate to get back to my video games, but he had this quiet patience that made you feel like the most important person in the world. He’d ask my opinion on things I knew nothing about, and when I gave a half-hearted answer, he’d nod seriously, as if I’d just uttered a profound truth. That was his gift: making you feel seen.

After he died, I started collecting these moments, piecing them together like a quilt. I’d lie awake at night and replay conversations, searching for hidden meanings, for things left unsaid. There was a day at the beach when I was eight, and he taught me to float on my back in the ocean. “Trust the water,” he said, his hand light under my spine. “It wants to hold you up.” I was terrified, certain I’d sink, but his voice was so calm, so absolutely certain, that I let go. And I floated. I remember looking up at the sky, an impossible blue, and hearing his laugh of pure delight. That laugh. It’s the first thing I forgot. Not the memory of it, but the actual sound. It’s been three years now, and I can’t hear it anymore, no matter how hard I try. I can see his face crinkle, the way his shoulders shook, but the sound itself is gone, replaced by a kind of silence that feels like betrayal.

I think we’re all just collections of these small things, these tiny habits and preferences that add up to a person. My mother has a way of humming when she’s concentrating, a tuneless vibration that I used to find maddening. Now I catch myself doing it when I’m deep in thought. My sister has his same walk, that slight drag, even though she never injured her foot. It’s as if his body’s memory passed into her, a ghost in the muscles. We carry each other forward in ways we don’t even notice.

Yesterday, I was in line at a coffee shop, and the man in front of me was wearing a jacket just like one my father owned, a brown corduroy thing with elbow patches. I almost said something. I wanted to tap him on the shoulder and ask where he got it, if it had that same faint smell of pipe tobacco, even though my father never smoked. But I didn’t. I just stood there, breathing in the scent of coffee and burnt sugar, and felt the weight of missing him settle into my chest like a stone into a riverbed.

There’s a tree outside my window now, a maple, and it’s starting to turn. Fall comes faster every year, or maybe I just pay more attention. He loved autumn. He’d rake leaves into giant piles, not to bag them, but just so we could jump into them. I remember the scratchy feel of dried leaves against my skin, the earthy smell of decay that is somehow not sad but comforting. One year, I was sixteen and too cool for such things. I sat on the porch, earbuds in, scrolling through my phone, while he raked alone. He didn’t say anything. He just kept raking, occasionally glancing over at me with this little half-smile that I now understand meant “I know you’re growing up, and that’s okay, even if it breaks my heart a little.”

I threw those earbuds away last year, found them in an old drawer and just tossed them in the trash. Silly, I know. They weren’t anything special, just cheap plastic things with a tangled cord. But they represented a wall I’d put up, a barrier between his world and mine. I wish I’d jumped in the leaves that day. I wish I’d made him a cup of coffee, even though he’d have added too much sugar himself anyway. I wish I’d asked him about the song he was always humming. I never did find out what it was.

Now I’m left with this: a kitchen table full of light, a chair that’s empty, and the faint echo of a tapping rhythm that only I can feel in the floorboards. I’ve started writing things down, a list of all the details I can remember. The way he’d say “well, well, well” when he was stalling for time. His obsession with perfectly sharpened pencils. The fact that he could never pass a stray dog without stopping. The list is long, but it will never be complete. There will always be gaps, moments that slipped through the cracks. That’s the cruelty of it. You don’t know what you’re losing until it’s gone, and by then, you’re just picking up shards, trying to glue them into something that resembles a whole.

But maybe that’s enough. Maybe we’re not meant to hold onto everything. Maybe the fading is part of the process, a way of making room for new things, new loves, new losses. Still, I’d give almost anything for one more afternoon in a hardware store, one more lesson on washers and bolts. I’d pay attention this time. I’d ask questions. I’d hold his hand when he reached for mine, even in public, even if someone from school might see. Because now I know that love isn’t in the grand gestures; it’s in the screwdriver aisle on a random Tuesday, in the way a person explains the world to a child who isn’t really listening but remembers anyway, years later, when it’s far too late to say thank you.

So I sit here, in the morning light, and I listen. The house settles. The refrigerator hums. Outside, the maple drops a single leaf, bright red, spiraling down to the grass. And I think, this is what remains: not the words, but the silence they leave behind, the space where a voice used to be, the shape of a hand that let go so I could float. It’s not a sound anymore, but a feeling, a pressure in the air, like music you can’t quite hear but know is playing somewhere, just beyond the edge of perception. And I carry it. I carry him. In the way I make my coffee, too sweet. In the way I tap my fingers on the counter without realizing it. In the way I stop for every stray dog. He’s gone, but he’s everywhere, scattered like seeds, growing in the cracks of my ordinary days, turning them into something close to sacred.

I think I finally understand. The small things aren’t small at all. They’re the whole thing. They’re the only thing. And maybe that’s enough.

message.reasoning: We are asked: "You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you." So I need to produce a piece of writing that is exactly 1000 words long. I can write about anything. It's a free writing exercise. I'll let my thoughts flow naturally, perhaps crafting a narrative, an essay, or a stream of consciousness. I should aim for exactly 1000 words. I'll write a piece that feels complete, maybe a short story or a reflective essay. I'll count words as I go to ensure it's exactly 1000. I'll write in a coherent manner, not just random words. I'll write a story about a moment of realization, perhaps. Let me start.
